policy_statement: Enable building design for disassembly and reuse by 2035.
mechanism_description: Enable design-for-disassembly and reuse in buildings so materials and components can be recovered and reused, reducing new material production and associated construction emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building design & construction practices → ↑ component recovery/reuse → ↓ virgin material production → ↓ industrial/process + energy emissions → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Building design, procurement, and construction methods are modified to enable disassembly (standardized connections, modularity, material passports) and to treat buildings as “material banks.”  
2) Mediating process: Higher rates of component salvage and material reuse reduce lifecycle demand for energy- and carbon-intensive virgin materials (notably cement/clinker, steel, aluminum), and can reduce construction waste processing.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower embodied emissions from upstream manufacturing and construction supply chains, yielding net reductions in city-associated GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing embodied GHG emissions by avoiding virgin material production through increased recovery and reuse of building components.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: DfD enables reuse, which primarily avoids new material production and thus reduces upstream industrial and process emissions (embodied carbon).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it also improves material efficiency and waste diversion, the climate-relevant outcome is chiefly GHG mitigation via avoided production emissions rather than hazard reduction (adaptation) or ecosystem carbon sinks (nature-based solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy frames DfD mainly as waste reduction, it still functions through avoided virgin production; if implemented poorly it could increase costs or transport, but the intended dominant pathway remains embodied-emissions reduction.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any “design for disassembly/buildings as material banks” policy inherently targets recoverability and reuse, which reduces demand for virgin materials and associated emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further subtyping (e.g., circular economy/resource efficiency co-benefit, waste policy linkage, or procurement vs. code pathway) requires policy-level specifics and is not set at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may primarily deliver “resource efficiency” outcomes (materials circularity) or could shift emissions via additional transport/storage of salvaged components; however, the dominant intended climate impact across this mechanism is embodied GHG mitigation from avoided virgin material production.
co_benefits: Waste reduction (less demolition waste), local circular-economy/job creation in deconstruction/salvage, potentially lower construction material costs over lifecycle.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Enable” is ambiguous about the mechanism (could later be implemented via building code changes, procurement requirements, or incentives). With only the sentence provided, it reads as a target/goal rather than a specified regulatory or spending instrument.
